title: CCPA Compliance
description: California Consumer Privacy Act compliance pattern. Implements consumer rights: right to know, right to delete, right to opt-out of sale, and right to non-discrimination. Includes privacy notices, data inventory, request handling workflows, and opt-out mechanisms. Required for businesses handling California residents' personal information.

provides.reasoning: Directly provides CCPA (California Consumer Privacy Act) compliance implementation, which is the core purpose of this pattern.
Implements consumer rights handling (right to know, delete, opt-out, non-discrimination) as specified in the pattern description with request handling workflows.
Provides the do-not-sell mechanism required by CCPA, including opt-out workflows to honor consumer requests to stop the sale of their data.
Pattern implements request handling workflows for consumer privacy requests, which is a key component mentioned in the description for processing all CCPA rights.
Pattern includes data inventory management as mentioned in the description, which is essential for CCPA compliance to track what personal information is collected.
Pattern includes privacy notices as mentioned in the description, which are required to inform consumers about their CCPA rights and data practices.

requires.description: Audit logs for CCPA compliance verification
requires.reasoning: CCPA compliance requires maintaining records of consumer requests and actions taken. Audit logging is essential for demonstrating compliance and handling potential disputes.
To fulfill 'right to know' and 'right to delete' requests, the pattern needs to discover where consumer data exists across systems, which is critical for CCPA compliance.
Must be able to identify and verify consumers making privacy requests to ensure requests are processed for the correct individual, which is foundational for consumer rights management.

retention_days.description: Data retention period in days. Shorter retention reduces compliance burden but may impact business analytics. CCPA requires disclosure of retention periods.

response_deadline_days.description: Maximum days to respond to consumer requests. CCPA legally requires 45 days maximum, with possible 45-day extension if needed.

verification_method.description: Method to verify consumer identity before responding to data requests. Higher verification prevents fraudulent requests but adds friction. CCPA requires reasonable verification.

do_not_sell_enabled.description: Enable 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link. Required if business sells personal information. Must be conspicuously posted.

data_inventory_tracking.description: How to track personal information categories and sources. Automated uses data discovery tools, manual requires documentation, hybrid combines both. Affects accuracy and maintenance burden.

request_workflow.description: How consumer requests are processed. Centralized uses single intake system, distributed routes to data owners, third-party uses compliance service. Affects response time and coordination.

opt_out_mechanism.description: Granularity of opt-out controls. Universal is simplest for consumers, per-category offers more control, per-third-party provides maximum transparency but complexity.

privacy_notice_placement.description: Where privacy notices are displayed. Collection-point notices improve transparency, modal ensures visibility but may impact user experience. CCPA requires notice at or before collection.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: CCPA compliance requires ongoing operational costs including: (1) Privacy management platform/software for data mapping, consent management, and request handling ($200-$2000/month depending on data volume and users), (2) Secure data storage and encryption for personal information inventory ($100-$1500/month for databases with encryption at rest/transit), (3) Audit logging and monitoring systems ($50-$500/month), (4) Automated request fulfillment infrastructure ($50-$1000/month for API gateways, workflow automation), (5) Identity verification services for consumer requests ($50-$500/month based on request volume), and (6) Compliance monitoring and reporting tools ($50-$2500/month). Minimum represents small business with <10K California consumers using basic tools; maximum represents enterprise with millions of consumers requiring comprehensive privacy infrastructure, multiple data systems integration, and dedicated compliance platforms like OneTrust or TrustArc.
provenance.adoptionCost: CCPA compliance is highly complex requiring: (1) Legal consultation to interpret requirements and create policies ($3000-$5000), (2) Comprehensive data inventory and mapping across all systems (40-80 hours at $150-$200/hour = $6000-$16000), (3) Implementation of privacy notices, cookie banners, and disclosure mechanisms (20-40 hours = $3000-$8000), (4) Building request handling workflows for know/delete/opt-out rights with verification processes (60-100 hours = $9000-$20000), (5) Staff training on consumer rights and request processing (10-20 hours = $1500-$4000), (6) Integration with existing systems for data retrieval and deletion (40-80 hours = $6000-$16000), and (7) Testing and documentation (20-40 hours = $3000-$8000). Total effort: 190-360 hours. Even with AI assistance reducing coding by 30%, legal complexity, cross-system integration, and business process changes remain substantial. Estimate assumes mid-size organization; enterprises may spend $50K-$200K.
provenance.licenseCost: CCPA compliance typically requires: (1) Privacy management platform license with perpetual or annual costs - options include OneTrust ($5000-$15000 first year), BigID ($8000-$20000), or TrustArc ($6000-$25000) depending on data volumes and features needed, (2) Data discovery and classification tools if not included ($2000-$10000), (3) Consent management platform ($1000-$5000), and (4) Compliance monitoring tools ($1000-$5000). While open-source alternatives exist (Apache Atlas for data catalog, custom-built consent systems), production-grade CCPA compliance at scale typically requires commercial privacy platforms for comprehensive data mapping, automated request fulfillment, and audit trails. Estimate represents entry-level commercial solution; enterprises often spend $20K-$100K+ annually on privacy tech stack.

supports_nfr.reason: This pattern is only selected when CCPA compliance is explicitly required.

requires_nfr.reason: CCPA compliance processes can support up to four nines availability as they involve workflow orchestration rather than real-time processing.
CCPA request processing (deletion, export, opt-out) requires workflow orchestration and data discovery, establishing a minimum 100ms p95 latency floor.
CCPA compliance workflows involve data discovery and batch processing across multiple data stores, establishing a 500ms p99 latency floor.
CCPA compliance systems require coordination across data inventory and request handling workflows, establishing a 15-minute RTO floor.
CCPA audit logs and request tracking require persistence with minimum 5-minute RPO to ensure consumer rights request durability.
CCPA request processing involves batch workflows and data discovery operations, limiting throughput to 360K jobs per hour (100 jobs/second).
CCPA mandates audit logging for all consumer rights requests and data processing activities to demonstrate compliance.
CCPA by definition applies only to systems handling personal information of California residents.

supports_constraints.reason: CCPA compliance requirements are cloud-agnostic and can be implemented on any infrastructure platform.
CCPA compliance is a cross-cutting operational/security pattern that applies regardless of programming language choice.
CCPA compliance applies to any platform that collects, processes, or stores personal information of California residents.

requires_constraints.reason: CCPA requires asynchronous processing of data subject requests (deletion, data export, opt-out) through workflow orchestration.
